Guideline: Initialize Fastify with TypeScript, logger, and ajv options
Description: Create a Fastify instance with proper TypeScript generics, Pino logger enabled, and Ajv configured for request validation with removeAdditional and coerceTypes.
Do: Use Fastify generic with typed server and enable logger with appropriate log level and Ajv options
Dont: Don't create Fastify without logger or with default Ajv settings that allow extra properties through

Guideline: Register plugins with fastify-plugin for proper encapsulation
Description: Wrap shared plugins with fastify-plugin (fp) so decorators and hooks are exposed to the parent context instead of being encapsulated inside the plugin scope.
Do: Wrap reusable plugins with fp() and set a unique name for debugging
Dont: Don't register shared plugins without fp() or they will be scoped and invisible to sibling routes

Guideline: Define typed routes with RouteGenericInterface
Description: Use the RouteGenericInterface to define Body, Querystring, Params, Headers, and Reply types for full type safety in route handlers.
Do: Define an interface extending RouteGenericInterface and pass it as a generic to the route method
Dont: Don't use untyped route handlers where req.body and req.params are unknown

Guideline: Use route shorthand methods for cleaner code
Description: Prefer shorthand methods like .get(), .post(), .put(), .delete() over the verbose .route() method for simple endpoints. Reserve .route() for complex configurations.
Do: Use shorthand methods with inline schema and handler for concise route definitions
Dont: Don't use .route() for simple CRUD endpoints where shorthands suffice

Guideline: JSON Schema for request and response validation
Description: Define JSON Schema objects for body, querystring, params, and response to enable automatic request validation and response serialization by Fastify.
Do: Attach schema objects to route options for automatic validation with descriptive error messages
Dont: Don't validate manually inside handlers or skip validation entirely

Guideline: Use @sinclair/typebox for type-safe schemas
Description: TypeBox generates JSON Schema objects that also produce TypeScript types, giving compile-time safety and runtime validation from a single schema definition.
Do: Define schemas with Type.Object() and extract static types with Static<typeof schema>
Dont: Don't maintain separate TypeScript interfaces and JSON Schema objects that can drift out of sync

Guideline: Use onRequest and preHandler lifecycle hooks
Description: onRequest runs before parsing and is ideal for authentication checks. preHandler runs after validation and is suitable for authorization and data enrichment logic.
Do: Use onRequest for auth token verification and preHandler for role-based access control
Dont: Don't put auth logic directly in route handlers or use the wrong hook for the task

Guideline: Use onSend and onError hooks for response transformation
Description: onSend allows modifying the serialized payload before sending. onError provides centralized error logging and transformation without replacing the error handler.
Do: Use onSend to add response headers or transform payloads and onError for structured error logging
Dont: Don't modify responses inside individual route handlers when the transformation applies globally

Guideline: Extend Fastify instance with custom decorators
Description: Use fastify.decorate() to attach shared services like database connections, caches, or utility functions to the Fastify instance for access across all routes.
Do: Use decorate() with proper TypeScript module augmentation so the decorator is typed
Dont: Don't attach properties directly to the fastify object or use global variables for shared services

Guideline: Use request and reply decorators for shared utilities
Description: decorateRequest() and decorateReply() add properties or methods to every request or reply object, useful for parsed user data, request timing, or response helpers.
Do: Use decorateRequest with null initial value and populate in hooks for per-request data
Dont: Don't store per-request state on the Fastify instance or use global mutable variables

Guideline: Use response schemas for fast JSON serialization
Description: Fastify uses fast-json-stringify when a response schema is defined, which is 2-5x faster than JSON.stringify(). Always define response schemas for performance-critical routes.
Do: Define response schemas with all expected properties so fast-json-stringify can optimize serialization
Dont: Don't omit response schemas on high-throughput endpoints or return unschematized objects

Guideline: Set a centralized error handler with setErrorHandler
Description: Use setErrorHandler to catch all errors, format consistent error responses, log errors, and map known error types to appropriate HTTP status codes.
Do: Use setErrorHandler with typed error responses and proper status code mapping for all error types
Dont: Don't let Fastify's default error handler expose raw error messages or stack traces to clients

Guideline: Create custom error classes with statusCode property
Description: Define custom error classes that include a statusCode property so Fastify automatically uses the correct HTTP status code in the error response.
Do: Create error classes extending Error with statusCode and use them in route handlers
Dont: Don't throw plain Error objects or use reply.code() manually for every error case in handlers

Guideline: Use @fastify/jwt plugin for JWT authentication
Description: Register @fastify/jwt to add jwt.sign() and jwt.verify() methods to the Fastify instance and request.jwtVerify() for route-level token verification.
Do: Register @fastify/jwt with a secret and use request.jwtVerify() in auth hooks
Dont: Don't implement JWT parsing manually or store secrets in code instead of environment variables

Guideline: Use preHandler hook for route-level authentication guards
Description: Create reusable authentication middleware as a preHandler hook function that can be applied selectively to routes or route groups that require authentication.
Do: Create a reusable authenticate function and apply it via preHandler on protected routes
Dont: Don't duplicate auth verification logic in each route handler or apply auth globally when some routes should be public

Guideline: Configure @fastify/cors plugin properly
Description: Register @fastify/cors with explicit origin, methods, and credentials settings instead of using permissive defaults that allow any origin.
Do: Specify exact allowed origins, methods, and headers in the CORS configuration
Dont: Don't use origin: true or origin: '*' in production as it allows any domain to make requests

Guideline: Integrate Prisma via a Fastify decorator plugin
Description: Register Prisma as a decorated property on the Fastify instance with proper connection lifecycle management including graceful disconnect on server close.
Do: Create a Prisma plugin with fp() that decorates the instance and handles onClose cleanup
Dont: Don't import PrismaClient directly in route files or create multiple client instances

Guideline: Use inject() method for integration testing
Description: Fastify's inject() method sends fake HTTP requests without starting a real server, enabling fast and reliable integration tests that exercise the full request lifecycle.
Do: Use app.inject() with method, url, payload, and headers to test routes without network overhead
Dont: Don't start a real server with listen() in tests or use external HTTP clients for integration tests

Guideline: Separate app builder for testable configuration
Description: Export a buildApp factory function that creates and configures the Fastify instance so tests can create isolated instances with different configurations.
Do: Export a buildApp() function that returns a configured Fastify instance for both production and testing
Dont: Don't export a singleton app instance that carries state between tests

Guideline: Configure built-in Pino logger with appropriate settings
Description: Fastify uses Pino for high-performance structured logging. Configure log level per environment, use serializers for sensitive data, and add request context automatically.
Do: Configure Pino with environment-based log level, custom serializers, and redaction paths
Dont: Don't use console.log for application logging or disable the logger in production

Guideline: Extend FastifyInstance interface with module augmentation
Description: Use TypeScript module augmentation to add type definitions for custom decorators, ensuring type safety when accessing decorated properties throughout the application.
Do: Declare module 'fastify' augmentations in a dedicated types file and import it early
Dont: Don't use type assertions (as any) to access custom decorators or skip type definitions

Guideline: Use Type Providers for end-to-end type safety
Description: Configure a Type Provider (TypeBox or Zod) at the Fastify instance level so route schemas automatically infer request and response types without manual generics.
Do: Register a type provider and use withTypeProvider() for automatic schema-to-type inference
Dont: Don't manually duplicate types as both TypeScript interfaces and JSON Schema when a type provider can unify them

Guideline: Configure connection pooling and keep-alive settings
Description: Tune Fastify's HTTP server keep-alive, connection timeout, and request timeout settings for production workloads to optimize throughput and resource usage.
Do: Set keepAliveTimeout, connectionTimeout, and requestTimeout based on your deployment environment
Dont: Don't use default timeout values in production or set excessively long timeouts that waste resources

Guideline: Use @fastify/autoload for automatic plugin registration
Description: @fastify/autoload scans directories and registers all plugins automatically with path-based route prefixing, eliminating manual plugin registration boilerplate.
Do: Use autoload with dir path to automatically register plugins and routes from directories
Dont: Don't manually import and register every plugin and route file as the application grows

Guideline: Implement graceful shutdown with close listeners
Description: Handle SIGINT and SIGTERM signals to gracefully close the Fastify server, allowing in-flight requests to complete and cleaning up database connections before exiting.
Do: Listen for process signals and call app.close() which triggers onClose hooks for all registered plugins
Dont: Don't call process.exit() immediately or ignore shutdown signals which can corrupt in-flight requests
